cards.grammarNote: In Korean, body and health conditions are expressed with adjective verbs: 아프다 (to be sick/hurt) → 아파요. These conjugate like regular verbs — there is no separate 'to be'.
아프다 → 아파요. ㅡ-irregular: drop ㅡ, add -아요 (preceding vowel ㅏ). 어디 아파요? = Where does it hurt?
괜찮다 → 괜찮아요. ㅎ-irregular: ㅎ drops before -아요. 괜찮아요? = Are you okay? Very common in both health and everyday contexts.
피곤하다 → 피곤해요. 하다 adjective verb. 많이 피곤해요 = very tired. 너무 피곤해요 = too tired.
불편하다 → 불편해요. 하다 adjective. 편해요 = comfortable/convenient (편하다). 불편해요 = uncomfortable.
무섭다 → 무서워요. ㅂ-irregular. 무서워요 = it's scary. Common when afraid of needles or procedures.

cards.front: I have a fever. / It's hot.
cards.grammarNote: 열이 나다 = to have a fever (lit: fever comes out). 열 = fever. 이 = subject marker. 나다 = to occur/come out.
붓다 (to swell) → 부어요. ㅅ-irregular: ㅅ drops before vowel endings. 얼굴이 부었어요 = my face is swollen.
심하다 (to be severe/serious) → 심해요. 하다 adjective → 해요. 많이 심해요 = very serious. 심하지 않아요 = it's not serious.
어지럽다 (to be dizzy) → 어지러워요. ㅂ-irregular: ㅂ → 우. 어지러워요 = I'm dizzy. 머리가 어지러워요 = my head is spinning.
메스껍다 → 메스꺼워요. ㅂ-irregular. 속이 안 좋아요 (my stomach is upset) is also very common.
어떻다 (how is it / what is it like) → 어때요. ㅎ-irregular: ㅎ drops before -아요. 지금 = now. 어때요? = How is it? How are you feeling?
